findings.title: Incorrect `sharesInEscrow` flag in `_postLiquidation` causes reward debt underflow and permanent DoS
findings.description:
In `_postLiquidation`, the `didTokenize` boolean indicates whether the liquidator's received shares were tokenized into a withdraw request (i.e., placed in escrow). However, this same `didTokenize` flag is erroneously passed as the `sharesInEscrow` parameter when updating the rewards for the `liquidateAccount`.

If `didTokenize` is true, the reward update for `liquidateAccount` will short-circuit (because `sharesInEscrow` is true and `accountSharesAfter > 0`). This leaves the `liquidateAccount`'s `rewardDebt` at its previous, higher value (based on their pre-liquidation balance). When the `liquidateAccount` later attempts to claim rewards, withdraw, or is liquidated again, the reward calculation `((accountSharesBefore * rewardsPerVaultShare) / DEFAULT_PRECISION) - rewardDebt` will underflow and revert. This results in a permanent Denial of Service for the `liquidateAccount`, preventing them from ever exiting their remaining position or being further liquidated.
findings.recommendation: Pass `_isWithdrawRequestPending(liquidateAccount)` instead of `didTokenize` for the `liquidateAccount`'s reward update.

findings.title: `executeUpgrade` does not clear upgrade state, allowing repeated executions and timelock bypass
findings.description:
In `TimelockUpgradeableProxy`, the `executeUpgrade` function upgrades the proxy to `newImplementation` and executes a delegatecall with the provided `data`. However, it does not reset `newImplementation` or `upgradeValidAt` after execution.

This allows the `upgradeAdmin` to call `executeUpgrade` multiple times after the initial 7-day timelock has passed. Since `executeUpgrade` forwards arbitrary `data` to the implementation via `delegatecall`, the admin can use this to execute any function on the implementation at any time without waiting for a new timelock. This breaks the 7-day timelock security guarantee, acting as a permanent backdoor for the admin to bypass the timelock for subsequent calls.
findings.recommendation: Reset `newImplementation` to `address(0)` and `upgradeValidAt` to `0` at the end of the `executeUpgrade` function.

findings.title: Vault bricking risk if yield token balance decreases below accrued fees
findings.description:
The `convertSharesToYieldToken` and `convertYieldTokenToShares` functions subtract `feesAccrued()` from `_yieldTokenBalance()`. The `feesAccrued()` value monotonically increases over time based on the `feeRate`.

If the underlying yield token experiences a loss (e.g., due to slashing, a negative rebase, or a hack in the underlying protocol), `_yieldTokenBalance()` may drop below `feesAccrued()`. If this happens, the subtraction will underflow, causing all core vault functions (`price`, `mintShares`, `burnShares`, `redeemNative`, `initiateWithdraw`) to revert. This results in a complete lock of user funds, preventing them from exiting the strategy even at a loss.

findings.title: Unauthenticated Morpho Callbacks Allow Complete Theft of User Funds and Router Balances
findings.description:
The `MorphoLendingRouter` implements Morpho Blue callbacks (`onMorphoFlashLoan`, `onMorphoRepay`, `onMorphoLiquidate`) but fails to verify that these callbacks were actually initiated by the router itself. An attacker can directly call `flashLoan`, `repay`, or `liquidate` on the Morpho contract and pass arbitrary `data` to trigger these callbacks.

In `onMorphoRepay`, an attacker can pass a victim's address as `sharesOwner` and their own address as `receiver`. The router will withdraw the victim's collateral, burn it for assets, and transfer the assets to the attacker, leading to a complete loss of funds for any user who has authorized the router.

In `onMorphoFlashLoan`, an attacker can pass a malicious contract as `migrateFrom`. The router will approve this malicious contract to spend its asset balance, allowing the attacker to steal any tokens held by the router.
findings.recommendation: Implement a mechanism to ensure callbacks are only processed when expected. For example, set a state variable before initiating a Morpho action and verify/clear it in the callback, or check that the callback data matches an expected hash.

findings.title: `forceWithdraw` Allows Liquidators to Specify Arbitrary Slippage and Exchange Data, Leading to Loss of User Funds
findings.description:
The `forceWithdraw` function allows anyone to initiate a withdrawal for an account if its health factor is below the threshold. The caller can pass arbitrary `data` which is forwarded to the vault's `initiateWithdraw` function. For single-sided LP vaults, this `data` contains `minAmounts` and `withdrawData` (which includes exchange data for trades).

A malicious liquidator can pass `minAmounts = 0` and malicious exchange data to sandwich the withdrawal or route trades through attacker-controlled pools. Because the liquidator controls the slippage limits and routing data for the user's forced withdrawal, they can steal the user's collateral during the withdrawal process.
findings.recommendation: Do not allow the caller of `forceWithdraw` to specify arbitrary `data`. Instead, the protocol should enforce strict slippage controls (e.g., using an oracle) or require the withdrawal to be purely proportional without any secondary trades.

findings.title: Liquidations Will Always Revert for Curve V2 Pools Due to Insufficient LP Token Balance
findings.description:
In `CurveConvex2Token._checkReentrancyContext`, the vault makes an external call to `CurveConvexLib.checkReentrancyContext()`. For Curve V2 pools, this function attempts to call `remove_liquidity(1, ...)` to check for reentrancy.

However, because this is an external call (not a `delegatecall`), the `msg.sender` to the Curve pool is the `CurveConvexLib` contract itself, which holds 0 LP tokens. Consequently, the `remove_liquidity(1, ...)` call will revert due to insufficient balance. Since `_checkReentrancyContext` is called during `_preLiquidation`, this will cause all liquidations for Curve V2 vaults to revert, leading to bad debt.
findings.recommendation: Execute `checkReentrancyContext` via `_delegateCall` so that it runs in the context of the vault, which holds the LP tokens. Alternatively, mint or transfer 1 wei of LP token to the `CurveConvexLib` contract during deployment.

findings.title: Withdraw Request Flow Reverts for Pools Containing Tokens Without a Withdraw Request Manager
findings.description:
In `BaseLPLib`, the functions `initiateWithdraw`, `getWithdrawRequestValue`, and `finalizeAndRedeemWithdrawRequest` iterate over all tokens in the LP pool and attempt to interact with their respective `WithdrawRequestManager`.

However, they fail to check if a manager actually exists (`address(manager) != address(0)`) for each token. If the pool contains a liquid token (e.g., WETH) that does not require a withdrawal manager, these functions will attempt to call `address(0)`, causing the entire transaction to revert. This completely breaks the withdrawal and liquidation flows for any LP vault that pairs an illiquid token with a liquid one.
findings.recommendation: Add checks for `address(manager) == address(0)` in `initiateWithdraw`, `getWithdrawRequestValue`, and `finalizeAndRedeemWithdrawRequest`. For tokens without a manager, the liquid assets should be held in escrow or immediately transferred, and their value should be calculated directly rather than querying a manager.

findings.title: Missing `receive()` function causes ETH transfers to revert
findings.description: `DineroWithdrawRequestManager` and `EtherFiWithdrawRequestManager` both interact with protocols that send native ETH to the caller. Specifically, `WETH.withdraw(amount)` is called in `_stakeTokens` for both contracts, which unwraps WETH and sends native ETH to `address(this)`. Additionally, `WithdrawRequestNFT.claimWithdraw(requestId)` in `EtherFiWithdrawRequestManager` and `PirexETH.redeemWithUpxEth` in `DineroWithdrawRequestManager` send ETH to the caller during withdrawal finalization. However, neither these contracts nor their base contract `AbstractWithdrawRequestManager` implement a `receive()` or `fallback()` payable function. As a result, any incoming ETH transfer will revert, completely breaking the staking and withdrawal finalization flows for these managers.
findings.recommendation: Add a `receive() external payable {}` function to `AbstractWithdrawRequestManager` or to the specific child contracts that require it to accept native ETH.

findings.title: DineroWithdrawRequestManager pools upxETH balances, allowing the first finalizer to steal ETH from others in the same batch
findings.description: In `DineroWithdrawRequestManager._finalizeWithdrawImpl`, the contract iterates over the batch IDs associated with a `requestId` and checks the contract's total balance of `upxETH` for each batch using `upxETH.balanceOf(address(this), i)`. It then redeems this entire balance. Because `upxETH` is minted to `address(this)` for all users, if multiple users initiate withdrawals that fall into the same batch ID, their `upxETH` balances are pooled together in the contract. The first user to call `finalizeAndRedeemWithdrawRequest` for that batch will redeem the entire pooled balance, and `tokensClaimed` will include the ETH belonging to all users in that batch. This ETH will be attributed entirely to the first user's `requestId`, leaving subsequent users with 0 tokens claimed and effectively stealing their funds.
findings.recommendation: Instead of relying on `upxETH.balanceOf(address(this), i)`, the contract must record the exact amount of `upxETH` minted (or `pxETH` redeemed) for each specific `requestId` during `_initiateWithdrawImpl`. During finalization, it should only redeem that specific recorded amount for the given batch.

findings.title: EthenaCooldownHolder calculates 0 tokens claimed if cooldown duration is 0, locking user funds
findings.description: In `EthenaCooldownHolder._startCooldown`, if `sUSDe.cooldownDuration() == 0`, the contract immediately redeems `sUSDe` for `USDe`, and the `USDe` is held in the `EthenaCooldownHolder` contract. Later, when `_finalizeCooldown` is called, it calculates `tokensClaimed = balanceAfter - balanceBefore`. Since `userCooldown.cooldownEnd` is 0, `sUSDe.unstake` is not called, meaning `balanceAfter` equals `balanceBefore`. As a result, `tokensClaimed` is calculated as 0, and 0 `USDe` is transferred to the manager. The actual `USDe` redeemed during `_startCooldown` remains permanently stuck in the `EthenaCooldownHolder` contract, and the user receives nothing.
findings.recommendation: Change the `tokensClaimed` calculation in `_finalizeCooldown` to simply use the total `USDe` balance of the holder contract, i.e., `tokensClaimed = USDe.balanceOf(address(this));`, since the cloned holder is dedicated to a single withdrawal request and all its `USDe` belongs to that request.

===FINDING===
TITLE: Incorrect `sharesInEscrow` flag in `_postLiquidation` causes reward debt underflow and permanent DoS
SEVERITY: high
CONFIDENCE: high
CATEGORY: Logic Error
FILE: src/rewards/RewardManagerMixin.sol
LINES: 68-74
DESCRIPTION: In `_postLiquidation`, the `didTokenize` boolean indicates whether the liquidator's received shares were tokenized into a withdraw request (i.e., placed in escrow). However, this same `didTokenize` flag is erroneously passed as the `sharesInEscrow` parameter when updating the rewards for the `liquidateAccount`. 

If `didTokenize` is true, the reward update for `liquidateAccount` will short-circuit (because `sharesInEscrow` is true and `accountSharesAfter > 0`). This leaves the `liquidateAccount`'s `rewardDebt` at its previous, higher value (based on their pre-liquidation balance). When the `liquidateAccount` later attempts to claim rewards, withdraw, or is liquidated again, the reward calculation `((accountSharesBefore * rewardsPerVaultShare) / DEFAULT_PRECISION) - rewardDebt` will underflow and revert. This results in a permanent Denial of Service for the `liquidateAccount`, preventing them from ever exiting their remaining position or being further liquidated.
RECOMMENDATION: Pass `_isWithdrawRequestPending(liquidateAccount)` instead of `didTokenize` for the `liquidateAccount`'s reward update.
===END===

===FINDING===
TITLE: `executeUpgrade` does not clear upgrade state, allowing repeated executions and timelock bypass
SEVERITY: medium
CONFIDENCE: high
CATEGORY: Access Control
FILE: src/proxy/TimelockUpgradeableProxy.sol
LINES: 45-50
DESCRIPTION: In `TimelockUpgradeableProxy`, the `executeUpgrade` function upgrades the proxy to `newImplementation` and executes a delegatecall with the provided `data`. However, it does not reset `newImplementation` or `upgradeValidAt` after execution. 

This allows the `upgradeAdmin` to call `executeUpgrade` multiple times after the initial 7-day timelock has passed. Since `executeUpgrade` forwards arbitrary `data` to the implementation via `delegatecall`, the admin can use this to execute any function on the implementation at any time without waiting for a new timelock. This breaks the 7-day timelock security guarantee, acting as a permanent backdoor for the admin to bypass the timelock for subsequent calls.
RECOMMENDATION: Reset `newImplementation` to `address(0)` and `upgradeValidAt` to `0` at the end of the `executeUpgrade` function.
===END===

===FINDING===
TITLE: Vault bricking risk if yield token balance decreases below accrued fees
SEVERITY: medium
CONFIDENCE: high
CATEGORY: Logic Error
FILE: src/AbstractYieldStrategy.sol
LINES: 65-67
DESCRIPTION: The `convertSharesToYieldToken` and `convertYieldTokenToShares` functions subtract `feesAccrued()` from `_yieldTokenBalance()`. The `feesAccrued()` value monotonically increases over time based on the `feeRate`. 

If the underlying yield token experiences a loss (e.g., due to slashing, a negative rebase, or a hack in the underlying protocol), `_yieldTokenBalance()` may drop below `feesAccrued()`. If this happens, the subtraction will underflow, causing all core vault functions (`price`, `mintShares`, `burnShares`, `redeemNative`, `initiateWithdraw`) to revert. This results in a complete lock of user funds, preventing them from exiting the strategy even at a loss.
RECOMMENDATION: Cap the subtracted fees at the current balance to prevent underflow:
```solidity
uint256 availableBalance = _yieldTokenBalance();
uint256 fees = feesAccrued();
if (fees > availableBalance) fees = availableBalance;
return (shares * (availableBalance - fees + VIRTUAL_YIELD_TOKENS)) / effectiveSupply();
```
===END===

===FINDING===
TITLE: Unauthenticated Morpho Callbacks Allow Complete Theft of User Funds and Router Balances
SEVERITY: critical
CONFIDENCE: high
CATEGORY: Access Control
FILE: src/routers/MorphoLendingRouter.sol
LINES: 104-121
DESCRIPTION: The `MorphoLendingRouter` implements Morpho Blue callbacks (`onMorphoFlashLoan`, `onMorphoRepay`, `onMorphoLiquidate`) but fails to verify that these callbacks were actually initiated by the router itself. An attacker can directly call `flashLoan`, `repay`, or `liquidate` on the Morpho contract and pass arbitrary `data` to trigger these callbacks. 

In `onMorphoRepay`, an attacker can pass a victim's address as `sharesOwner` and their own address as `receiver`. The router will withdraw the victim's collateral, burn it for assets, and transfer the assets to the attacker, leading to a complete loss of funds for any user who has authorized the router.

In `onMorphoFlashLoan`, an attacker can pass a malicious contract as `migrateFrom`. The router will approve this malicious contract to spend its asset balance, allowing the attacker to steal any tokens held by the router.
RECOMMENDATION: Implement a mechanism to ensure callbacks are only processed when expected. For example, set a state variable before initiating a Morpho action and verify/clear it in the callback, or check that the callback data matches an expected hash.
===END===

===FINDING===
TITLE: `forceWithdraw` Allows Liquidators to Specify Arbitrary Slippage and Exchange Data, Leading to Loss of User Funds
SEVERITY: high
CONFIDENCE: high
CATEGORY: Logic Error
FILE: src/routers/AbstractLendingRouter.sol
LINES: 154-162
DESCRIPTION: The `forceWithdraw` function allows anyone to initiate a withdrawal for an account if its health factor is below the threshold. The caller can pass arbitrary `data` which is forwarded to the vault's `initiateWithdraw` function. For single-sided LP vaults, this `data` contains `minAmounts` and `withdrawData` (which includes exchange data for trades). 

A malicious liquidator can pass `minAmounts = 0` and malicious exchange data to sandwich the withdrawal or route trades through attacker-controlled pools. Because the liquidator controls the slippage limits and routing data for the user's forced withdrawal, they can steal the user's collateral during the withdrawal process.
RECOMMENDATION: Do not allow the caller of `forceWithdraw` to specify arbitrary `data`. Instead, the protocol should enforce strict slippage controls (e.g., using an oracle) or require the withdrawal to be purely proportional without any secondary trades.
===END===

===FINDING===
TITLE: Liquidations Will Always Revert for Curve V2 Pools Due to Insufficient LP Token Balance
SEVERITY: high
CONFIDENCE: high
CATEGORY: Denial of Service
FILE: src/single-sided-lp/CurveConvex2Token.sol
LINES: 68-70
DESCRIPTION: In `CurveConvex2Token._checkReentrancyContext`, the vault makes an external call to `CurveConvexLib.checkReentrancyContext()`. For Curve V2 pools, this function attempts to call `remove_liquidity(1, ...)` to check for reentrancy. 

However, because this is an external call (not a `delegatecall`), the `msg.sender` to the Curve pool is the `CurveConvexLib` contract itself, which holds 0 LP tokens. Consequently, the `remove_liquidity(1, ...)` call will revert due to insufficient balance. Since `_checkReentrancyContext` is called during `_preLiquidation`, this will cause all liquidations for Curve V2 vaults to revert, leading to bad debt.
RECOMMENDATION: Execute `checkReentrancyContext` via `_delegateCall` so that it runs in the context of the vault, which holds the LP tokens. Alternatively, mint or transfer 1 wei of LP token to the `CurveConvexLib` contract during deployment.
===END===

===FINDING===
TITLE: Withdraw Request Flow Reverts for Pools Containing Tokens Without a Withdraw Request Manager
SEVERITY: high
CONFIDENCE: high
CATEGORY: Logic Error
FILE: src/single-sided-lp/AbstractSingleSidedLP.sol
LINES: 266-281
DESCRIPTION: In `BaseLPLib`, the functions `initiateWithdraw`, `getWithdrawRequestValue`, and `finalizeAndRedeemWithdrawRequest` iterate over all tokens in the LP pool and attempt to interact with their respective `WithdrawRequestManager`. 

However, they fail to check if a manager actually exists (`address(manager) != address(0)`) for each token. If the pool contains a liquid token (e.g., WETH) that does not require a withdrawal manager, these functions will attempt to call `address(0)`, causing the entire transaction to revert. This completely breaks the withdrawal and liquidation flows for any LP vault that pairs an illiquid token with a liquid one.
RECOMMENDATION: Add checks for `address(manager) == address(0)` in `initiateWithdraw`, `getWithdrawRequestValue`, and `finalizeAndRedeemWithdrawRequest`. For tokens without a manager, the liquid assets should be held in escrow or immediately transferred, and their value should be calculated directly rather than querying a manager.
===END===

===FINDING===
TITLE: Missing `receive()` function causes ETH transfers to revert
SEVERITY: high
CONFIDENCE: high
CATEGORY: logic error
FILE: src/withdraws/AbstractWithdrawRequestManager.sol
LINES: 49-53
DESCRIPTION: `DineroWithdrawRequestManager` and `EtherFiWithdrawRequestManager` both interact with protocols that send native ETH to the caller. Specifically, `WETH.withdraw(amount)` is called in `_stakeTokens` for both contracts, which unwraps WETH and sends native ETH to `address(this)`. Additionally, `WithdrawRequestNFT.claimWithdraw(requestId)` in `EtherFiWithdrawRequestManager` and `PirexETH.redeemWithUpxEth` in `DineroWithdrawRequestManager` send ETH to the caller during withdrawal finalization. However, neither these contracts nor their base contract `AbstractWithdrawRequestManager` implement a `receive()` or `fallback()` payable function. As a result, any incoming ETH transfer will revert, completely breaking the staking and withdrawal finalization flows for these managers.
RECOMMENDATION: Add a `receive() external payable {}` function to `AbstractWithdrawRequestManager` or to the specific child contracts that require it to accept native ETH.
===END===

===FINDING===
TITLE: DineroWithdrawRequestManager pools upxETH balances, allowing the first finalizer to steal ETH from others in the same batch
SEVERITY: critical
CONFIDENCE: high
CATEGORY: logic error
FILE: src/withdraws/Dinero.sol
LINES: 49-62
DESCRIPTION: In `DineroWithdrawRequestManager._finalizeWithdrawImpl`, the contract iterates over the batch IDs associated with a `requestId` and checks the contract's total balance of `upxETH` for each batch using `upxETH.balanceOf(address(this), i)`. It then redeems this entire balance. Because `upxETH` is minted to `address(this)` for all users, if multiple users initiate withdrawals that fall into the same batch ID, their `upxETH` balances are pooled together in the contract. The first user to call `finalizeAndRedeemWithdrawRequest` for that batch will redeem the entire pooled balance, and `tokensClaimed` will include the ETH belonging to all users in that batch. This ETH will be attributed entirely to the first user's `requestId`, leaving subsequent users with 0 tokens claimed and effectively stealing their funds.
RECOMMENDATION: Instead of relying on `upxETH.balanceOf(address(this), i)`, the contract must record the exact amount of `upxETH` minted (or `pxETH` redeemed) for each specific `requestId` during `_initiateWithdrawImpl`. During finalization, it should only redeem that specific recorded amount for the given batch.
===END===

===FINDING===
TITLE: EthenaCooldownHolder calculates 0 tokens claimed if cooldown duration is 0, locking user funds
SEVERITY: high
CONFIDENCE: high
CATEGORY: logic error
FILE: src/withdraws/Ethena.sol
LINES: 30-45
DESCRIPTION: In `EthenaCooldownHolder._startCooldown`, if `sUSDe.cooldownDuration() == 0`, the contract immediately redeems `sUSDe` for `USDe`, and the `USDe` is held in the `EthenaCooldownHolder` contract. Later, when `_finalizeCooldown` is called, it calculates `tokensClaimed = balanceAfter - balanceBefore`. Since `userCooldown.cooldownEnd` is 0, `sUSDe.unstake` is not called, meaning `balanceAfter` equals `balanceBefore`. As a result, `tokensClaimed` is calculated as 0, and 0 `USDe` is transferred to the manager. The actual `USDe` redeemed during `_startCooldown` remains permanently stuck in the `EthenaCooldownHolder` contract, and the user receives nothing.
RECOMMENDATION: Change the `tokensClaimed` calculation in `_finalizeCooldown` to simply use the total `USDe` balance of the holder contract, i.e., `tokensClaimed = USDe.balanceOf(address(this));`, since the cloned holder is dedicated to a single withdrawal request and all its `USDe` belongs to that request.
===END===
